content.text: What It Is

A cross-team dependency registry is a structured, living catalogue of every relationship between work items owned by different teams in an agentic AI programme where the progress of one team's deliverable is contingent on the completion of another's. In agentic AI contexts, dependencies arise at multiple layers: a reasoning agent may depend on a tool integration built by the platform team, an evaluation harness depends on a labelling pipeline owned by data operations, and a production deployment depends on a security review completed by an independent governance team. The registry makes all these relationships explicit, visible, and actively managed rather than discovered reactively when deadlines are missed.

Unlike a simple task list or project schedule, a dependency registry captures the full semantic context of each dependency: what exactly is needed, from whom, by when, what the consequence of a delay would be, and what mitigation is available if the dependency cannot be met on time. This richer metadata transforms the registry from a passive record into an active risk management tool that programme managers can use to identify and resolve dependency conflicts before they become delivery blockers.

Dependency registries in agentic AI programmes must handle a wider range of dependency types than traditional software projects. Model-level dependencies—a fine-tuned model variant required by a downstream agent—have different lead times and substitution options than infrastructure dependencies or process dependencies such as a required regulatory approval. The registry schema must accommodate this diversity while remaining simple enough that busy teams actually update it.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI programmes involve more cross-team interdependencies than typical software projects because they combine multiple technical disciplines—model development, infrastructure, data engineering, security, and product design—with organisational disciplines such as legal review, change management, and compliance. A missing dependency rarely fails silently: it typically blocks an entire workstream, cascades into multiple dependent teams, and surfaces as a crisis at the point of planned integration.

The cost of unmanaged dependencies compounds over time. A platform team that does not know another team is depending on their API assumes they can refactor it freely. A data engineering team unaware that a training pipeline is being consumed by three downstream workstreams plans their maintenance window without accounting for the impact. A dependency registry prevents these misalignments by making the web of interdependencies visible to everyone who needs to see it.

Programme managers who maintain active dependency registries spend significantly less time in reactive escalation and significantly more time in proactive coordination. Research on large-scale software delivery consistently shows that integration failures are the primary cause of schedule overruns, and the majority of integration failures are preceded by discoverable dependency misalignments that were not tracked.
Benefits
Early risk detection:
Dependencies with long lead times or uncertain delivery dates are surfaced at the start of the quarter rather than discovered at integration time.
Ownership clarity:
Every dependency has a named owner who is accountable for delivering it on time and communicating changes proactively.
Reduced integration failures:
Teams integrate against known, tracked interfaces rather than discovering incompatibilities at the last moment.
Informed capacity planning:
Teams can see which of their outputs are being depended on and plan accordingly, rather than being surprised by urgent requests.
Escalation efficiency:
When a dependency is at risk, the registry provides the context needed to escalate effectively, including impact assessment and mitigation options.
Audit trail:
Historical dependency records support post-mortem analysis and inform future programme planning.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Maintenance overhead:
A registry that teams do not actively update becomes stale and misleading. Keeping it current requires sustained discipline and tooling support.
False sense of security:
A complete-looking registry may conceal undiscovered dependencies that no one has thought to register yet, particularly in novel agentic architectures.
Rigidity risk:
Over-specifying dependencies early in a project can reduce team autonomy and slow adaptation to changing requirements.
Tooling proliferation:
Without standardised tooling, teams maintain dependency information in incompatible formats—spreadsheets, Jira custom fields, wiki pages—that cannot be aggregated for programme-level views.
Discovery lag:
Dependencies in exploratory work are often not known until relatively late in development, making early registration difficult and incomplete.
How It Works

A dependency registry operates as a shared data store—typically a database-backed tool, a structured spreadsheet, or a project management platform with custom fields—that each team queries and updates as their work evolves. The registry is populated through a combination of planned dependency identification during quarterly planning, ongoing registration as new dependencies emerge during execution, and retrospective discovery when teams realise post-hoc that they depended on something they had not tracked.

The registry uses a standard schema for each dependency entry. At minimum, a dependency entry includes a unique identifier, the dependent work item, the prerequisite work item, the providing team, the required delivery date, the current status, the confidence level, and the impact if the dependency is not met. Additional fields capture the dependency type, escalation contacts, mitigation options, and history of status changes.

Status transitions are the operative mechanism of the registry. When a dependency moves from green to amber—indicating a delivery risk—the providing team notifies the dependent team and proposes a revised timeline or a mitigation. When it moves to red—indicating a confirmed delay—an escalation process activates, bringing in the programme manager and potentially the sponsor to make resource or scope decisions. The transitions are tracked with timestamps so the programme manager can see how quickly risks were identified and resolved.
Architecture

The registry flow begins with dependency identification during quarterly planning and sprint kickoffs, where teams map their work items against shared infrastructure, shared models, and shared services. Identified dependencies are classified by type and scored by risk—a function of lead time, delivery confidence, and impact severity—then assigned to named owners who accept accountability for delivery. The tracking phase provides continuous visibility through automated status updates and owner-reported changes. Resolution closes the dependency with a delivery confirmation, while escalation routes unresolved high-risk dependencies to programme leadership for intervention.
When to Use It
Multi-team agentic AI programmes:
Any programme where three or more teams share infrastructure, models, data pipelines, or approval processes.
Quarterly planning:
Dependencies should be catalogued before commitments are locked so their risk is factored into team capacity planning.
Integration-heavy phases:
Quarters that include major integration milestones—model handoffs, API version upgrades, data schema migrations—warrant heightened dependency tracking.
New vendor or partner integrations:
External dependencies with longer lead times and less controllable delivery timelines are especially important to track.
Compliance-gated work:
Legal, security, or regulatory dependencies that could block production deployment need early registration and executive visibility.
How to Implement
Define the schema:
Agree on the minimum fields for a dependency entry and the status taxonomy before launching the registry. Minimise mandatory fields to reduce maintenance friction.
Choose the tooling:
Select a tool the teams already use—Jira, Linear, Notion, or a shared spreadsheet—rather than introducing a new system that adds adoption friction.
Run a dependency identification workshop:
At the start of each quarter, bring all team leads together for a two-hour session to map inter-team dependencies for the upcoming quarter's work.
Assign owners and due dates:
Every dependency gets a named owner and a required delivery date before the workshop closes.
Establish update cadence:
Define when and how teams update dependency status—weekly updates by owners, automated status sync where tooling supports it.
Review in programme syncs:
Include a dependency review as a standing agenda item in every programme-level meeting, focusing on amber and red items.
Run dependency retrospectives:
At quarter end, review which dependencies were missed, which were discovered late, and what process changes would improve early identification.
Examples

# dependency-registry-entry.yaml — canonical schema
dependencies:
  - id: dep-2025-q2-001
    title: "Semantic router model deployment"
    dependent_item:
      id: "AGENT-142"
      title: "Customer intent routing agent - production launch"
      team: customer-automation
    prerequisite_item:
      id: "PLAT-089"
      title: "Semantic router v2 deployed to inference cluster"
      team: platform-ml
    dependency_type: model  # model | infrastructure | data | process | external
    required_by: "2025-04-15"
    current_status: amber   # green | amber | red | resolved
    confidence: medium      # high | medium | low
    impact_if_delayed: |
      Customer automation team cannot launch routing agent in Q2.
      Affects three downstream workflows totalling 40 FTE hours/week saved.
    mitigation: |
      Use rule-based routing for top-10 intent categories as fallback.
      Covers 70% of volume but misses long-tail intents.
    providing_team_contact: "alice@example.com"
    dependent_team_contact: "bob@example.com"
    last_updated: "2025-03-28"
    status_history:
      - date: "2025-03-01"
        status: green
        note: "Model training on track"
      - date: "2025-03-21"
        status: amber
        note: "Evaluation revealed accuracy gap on multilingual inputs; retraining required"
Real-World Example

A retail financial services company building an automated loan assessment agent discovered mid-quarter that their credit risk model—provided by a separate analytics team—had a schema change that broke the agent's input parser. The change had not been tracked as a dependency because both teams assumed the schema was stable. The resulting two-week outage cost four sprint-days of unplanned remediation work and delayed the production launch by a full quarter.

After the incident, the programme implemented a cross-team dependency registry with mandatory registration for all shared data schemas, model APIs, and infrastructure components. The registry included a change-notification protocol: any team modifying a registered interface was required to post a changelog entry and notify all dependent teams at least ten business days before the change took effect. In the subsequent four quarters, zero unplanned integration failures occurred because of schema or API changes that had been registered.
Common Mistakes
Registering only outbound dependencies:
Teams track what they need from others but not what others need from them, leaving the registry incomplete and providing teams unaware of their consumers.
Treating the registry as a one-time artefact:
Dependencies evolve as work progresses. A registry that is populated at the start of a quarter and not updated becomes misleading by week four.
Skipping the dependency identification workshop:
Teams that rely on individuals to self-register dependencies miss the cross-team dependencies that no single team sees in isolation.
No escalation path for red dependencies:
Without a defined escalation process, red dependencies linger without resolution because no one knows who should act.
Excessive granularity:
Registering every minor task-level dependency creates maintenance overhead that overwhelms teams and obscures the high-impact dependencies that matter.
Best Practices
Register at the workstream level:
Track dependencies between significant deliverables—model versions, API contracts, pipeline outputs—not individual tasks.
Use three status levels:
Green, amber, and red are sufficient for programme visibility. More granular statuses add complexity without improving decision-making.
Automate status sync where possible:
If the team's delivery tool has API access, automate updates to the registry when linked tickets are completed.
Make the registry the source of truth for integration planning:
Integration milestone schedules should be derived from the registry, not maintained separately.
Reward early bad news:
Create explicit recognition for teams that flag amber dependencies early. Teams that wait until red before reporting are creating unnecessary programme risk.
Review dependency patterns quarterly:
Which teams are consistently providing critical dependencies? Are there structural bottlenecks that could be addressed through team restructuring or platform investment?
Performance and Scalability

Dependency registries scale poorly when implemented as monolithic spreadsheets maintained by a single programme manager. At ten teams and fifty dependencies, a spreadsheet is manageable. At twenty teams and two hundred dependencies, it becomes a bottleneck. Scalable implementations use a project management tool with custom dependency fields that each team owns and updates, with a programme-level dashboard aggregating the data in near real time.

For very large programmes—fifty or more teams—dependency management tools such as Jira's Dependency Matrix, Linear's dependency graph, or custom tooling built on a shared YAML schema and visualised through a lightweight web application provide the necessary scale. The key architectural principle is that teams are the authoritative source of dependency data, not a central registry team. Centralised registries become stale because the people closest to the work are not the ones updating the record.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Jira dependency links:
Native dependency tracking with blocker and is-blocked-by relationships, filterable at programme level through Jira's roadmap view.
Linear:
Modern project management tool with dependency graphs and cycle detection, popular with engineering-led teams.
Confluence + custom templates:
Lightweight option for teams already using Confluence; works well for programmes where dependencies are relatively stable and few in number.
Custom YAML + Git:
Version-controlled dependency registry with structured schema, suitable for teams that prefer tooling they control and can query programmatically.
Miro or Mural:
Visual dependency maps useful for workshops and stakeholder communication, though not suitable as a live operational registry.
Comparison with Alternatives
Ad-hoc email or Slack threads:
Produces no structured record, creates information silos, and leaves dependencies invisible to anyone not included in the original thread.
Project schedule dependencies in MS Project or Smartsheet:
More sophisticated than a registry but requires a dedicated programme manager to maintain and is often too rigid for agile delivery.
Dependency matrix in programme management tools:
Good for visibility but requires teams to maintain data in the tool consistently, which many teams resist.
Integrated dependency tracking in a DevOps platform:
Highest fidelity but requires upfront investment in tooling and process; most effective for mature programmes with established DevOps practices.
Security and Reliability

Dependency registries often contain sensitive information about system architecture, vendor relationships, and upcoming platform changes. Access should be controlled so that the registry is visible to all teams contributing to the programme but not publicly accessible. For programmes operating in regulated industries, the registry may also contain information about compliance-gated dependencies whose disclosure could have legal implications, warranting role-based access controls.

Reliability of the registry as a programme management tool depends on data accuracy. A registry whose status fields are not current is worse than no registry because it creates false confidence. Programme managers should validate registry accuracy at each programme sync by cross-checking a sample of green dependencies with the providing team's current status. Systematic inaccuracy should trigger a process review rather than individual blame.
Key Takeaways
A dependency registry makes inter-team dependencies explicit, owned, and actively managed rather than discovered reactively.
The schema must balance completeness with maintenance simplicity—register at the workstream level, not the task level.
A dependency identification workshop at the start of each quarter is more effective than relying on self-registration.
Status transitions from green to amber to red, with defined escalation paths at each level, are the operational mechanism that makes the registry useful.
The registry should be owned by the teams closest to the work, with programme managers providing the structure and the programme-level view.
Automating status updates where tooling supports it dramatically reduces the maintenance burden and improves data accuracy.
Automating Dependency Status Tracking

Manually updating a dependency registry is the primary source of registry staleness. When teams must remember to log into a separate tool and change a status field, the update lag grows throughout the quarter, and programme managers rely on stale data for their risk assessments. Automating status synchronisation between the registry and the teams' primary delivery tools dramatically reduces maintenance overhead and improves data currency.

The most effective automation strategy is to link registry dependency status to the completion state of specific tickets in the team's issue tracker. When a ticket that represents a dependency deliverable is moved to 'Done' in Jira or Linear, the registry status updates automatically to 'resolved.' When a ticket is moved back to 'In Progress' or a blocker label is added, the registry status updates to 'amber.' This bidirectional sync means that teams update the registry as a natural by-product of their normal work tracking, not as a separate administrative activity.

For dependencies that involve external parties—vendors, regulatory bodies, legal teams—automated status tracking is not feasible, but structured check-in schedules reduce the update lag. External dependencies should have a named internal owner who is responsible for obtaining status updates at a defined cadence—weekly for high-risk external dependencies, biweekly for lower-risk ones—and recording the current status in the registry. The programme manager's monitoring dashboard should surface external dependencies that have not been updated within their expected cadence, triggering a follow-up.

Beyond ticket-based sync, some organisations use webhook-based triggers to update dependency status in near real-time when specific events occur: a pull request merged into a protected branch, a deployment pipeline completing successfully, or a test suite achieving a coverage threshold. These event-driven updates provide even better status currency than polling-based sync and reduce the lag between a dependency being delivered and the programme manager seeing it as resolved.
Jira webhook integration:
Configure Jira to POST to a registry update endpoint when specific ticket events occur, enabling near-real-time status synchronisation.
CI/CD pipeline triggers:
Successful pipeline runs that deploy a dependency deliverable can automatically mark the dependency as resolved in the registry.
External dependency check-in schedule:
Named owners check in on external dependencies at a defined cadence; dashboard surfaces overdue check-ins for programme manager follow-up.
Status change notifications:
Automated notifications to the dependent team when a dependency status changes to amber or red, ensuring consuming teams know about risks as soon as they are identified.
Dependency Registry Metrics and Health Indicators

A dependency registry is itself a source of valuable programme health data. Aggregate metrics derived from the registry reveal patterns that individual dependency reviews miss: which teams are consistently the source of amber and red dependencies, which dependency types have the highest delay rates, how long it typically takes to resolve escalations, and how much early warning time exists between amber and red transitions.

A programme dashboard should surface four key dependency health metrics. The open amber and red count shows the current risk level at a glance—more than ten percent of dependencies in non-green status warrants immediate programme management attention. The average time to resolution for amber dependencies indicates whether the programme has effective escalation and resolution mechanisms. The late registration rate—the fraction of dependencies that were registered after the quarterly planning session—indicates how well teams are anticipating cross-team needs. The retrospective discovery rate—dependencies discovered post-mortem after causing an impact—indicates gaps in the dependency identification process.
Open amber/red count:
The number of active dependencies in non-green status; a leading indicator of programme delivery risk.
Average amber resolution time:
How many days, on average, it takes to resolve an amber dependency; indicates escalation effectiveness.
Late registration rate:
The fraction of dependencies registered after the quarterly planning session; indicates gaps in dependency identification.
Retrospective discovery rate:
The fraction of dependencies identified only after causing a delivery impact; the most important metric to drive to zero.
Dependency concentration by team:
Which teams are named as providers most often; teams with high dependency concentration are programme bottlenecks requiring capacity or architecture attention.
